q1: At Stripe, our merchant KYC activation had stalled at 38% for two quarters and engineering wanted to ship a new SSO project instead. I anchored the team on a 5-day discovery sprint, found that 60% of drop-off was on a single document-upload step, and led the team to redesign that step behind a 50/50 experiment. Activation moved from 38% to 51% in 6 weeks, a 13pp lift, and we replicated the pattern across two adjacent funnels.
q2: When I deprecated legacy webhooks across three partner teams, two engineering leads pushed back hard because their roadmaps would slip a quarter. I scheduled 1:1s with each, mapped their dependencies onto a shared timeline, offered a 4-week migration shim my team would own, and got buy-in within 8 days. We shipped the deprecation on schedule with zero partner-side incidents.
q3: When I joined Cash App for Business, the goal was 'grow onboarding,' which was too vague. I scoped it down to time-to-first-payment, built a funnel dashboard, and got the team aligned on cutting median TTFP by 30%. We ended up cutting it by 42% over two quarters by removing 3 friction steps and changing the default banking partner.
q4: We sunset a $4M/yr SMB lending pilot at Square. I'd championed it for 6 months, but the unit economics didn't work without a credit partnership we couldn't get. I led the 90-day discovery, wrote the kill memo, and reallocated the budget to a receivables product that did 3x the revenue in year one. The lesson: validate the partnership before validating the demand.
q5: I made the Connect onboarding redesign call almost entirely from data. We had session recordings showing 60% drop-off on document upload, support tickets clustering on the same step, and a cohort analysis showing those who got past it had 2x retention at 90 days. I deprioritized 2 stakeholder-favorite features to make room. The trade-off was visible team frustration short-term, offset by the activation lift.
q6: At Square I pushed to keep a refund-status API as a first-class endpoint when sales wanted it removed because it competed with their dashboard. I pulled 30 days of usage data showing 4 enterprise customers depended on it for their own merchant tooling. I lost the room twice but won on the third try by bringing one of those customers into the room. We kept the endpoint and added a paid SLA on top.
